segments.text: Part 6: Nucleotide Metabolism

DNA/RNA bases
Purines:
A & G; 2 rings; from:
Amino acids: Aspartate, Glycine, Glutamine
and Tetrahydrofolate
Pyrimidines :
T, C and U; 1 ring; from:
Aspartate and carbamoylphosphate

Purines and Pyrimidines
A binds to T/U
(2 H-bonds)
G binds to C
(3 H-bonds)

Question
A scientist isolates a double stranded DNA fragment from a volunteer. Upon analysis, she discovers that the fragment is 20% Guanosine , what is the % of Adenosine in the DNA fragment?
50%
40%
30%
20%
% G = % C
% A = % T
%A + %G +%C + %T = 100%
% G = % C = 20%
% A = % T = 30%

Nucleoside: Base + Ribose
Base	+ sugar = nucleoside
Adenine 	+ ribose = adenosine
Guanine 	+ ribose = guanosine
Cytosine	+ ribose = cytidine
Thymine 	+ ribose = thymidine
Uracil	+ ribose = uridine

Nucleotide vs. Nucleoside
What is the difference between a nucleotide and a nucleoside?

26.1 Can Cells Synthesize Nucleotides?
Nearly all organisms synthesize purines and pyrimidines de novo ( “ anew ” )
Many organisms also "salvage" purines and pyrimidines from diet and degradative pathways
Ribose of nucleotides can be a source of metabolic energy, but purine and pyrimidine rings cannot
Nucleotide synthesis pathways are good targets for anti-cancer/antibacterial strategies

Purine Synthesis
In all tissue
Starting with the sugar
The base is built piece by piece
IMP is intermediate for the synthesis of AMP or GMP
IMP: inosine monophosphate
XMP: Xanthosine monophosphate

For AMP synthesis:
amine comes from Aspartate ( fumarate is released)
process requires GTP
For GMP synthesis:
amine comes from glutamine (glutamate is released)
Process requires ATP

Purine synthesis regulation
GTP is the energy source for AMP synthesis,
ATP is the energy source for GMP synthesis
GMP inhibits IMP dehydrogenase (feedback inhibition)
AMP inhibits adenylosuccinate synthase (feedback inhibition)

Purine Synthesis

Regulation of purine synthesis
Ribose-5-phosphate
What?!!

IMP as a key intermediate
Inosine is the basic “purine” nucleotide structure
Modification leads to GMP or AMP
Inosine without the sugar is hypoxanthine
OR: hypoxanthine + sugar = inosine

Purine salvage
Synthesis of GMP or AMP from:
1. Hypoxanthine → IMP → GMP or AMP
2. Guanine → GMP
3. Adenine → AMP
HGPRT (hypoxanthine/guanine phosphoribosyl transferase )
HGPRT Deficiency: Lesch-Nyhan syndrome
Adenine phosphorybosyl transferase

HGPRT Converts Bases Back to Nucleotides
Figure 26.7 Purine salvage by the HGPRT reaction.  HGPRT is a purine phosphoribosyltransferase.

HGPRT Converts Bases Back to Nucleotides
Figure 26.7 Purine salvage by the HGPRT reaction.  HGPRT is a purine phosphoribosyltransferase.

Lesch-Nyhan
X-linked recessive
Deficiency in HPRT enzyme
Clinical picture:
Child, spastic behavior, Self-mutilation, intellectual disability , orange-red urine , Arthritis, kidney stones
Increased uric acid in blood and urine, a condition known as gout
Why?
Because purine degradation results in uric acid

26.5 How Do Cells Synthesize Pyrimidines?
In contrast to purines, pyrimidines are not synthesized as nucleotides
Rather, the pyrimidine ring is completed before a ribose-5-P is added
Carbamoyl-phosphate and aspartate are the precursors of the six atoms of the pyrimidine ring
Mammals have two enzymes for carbamoyl phosphate synthesis – carbamoyl phosphate for pyrimidine synthesis is formed by carbamoyl phosphate synthetase II (CPS-II) , a cytosolic enzyme

26.5 How Do Cells Synthesize Pyrimidines?
Figure 26.13 The metabolic origin of the six atoms of the pyrimidine ring. Only two precursors contribute atoms to the six-membered pyrimidine ring.

Carbamoyl phosphate synthetase II
Carbamoyl phosphate for pyrimidine synthesis is made by carbamoyl phosphate synthetase II (CPS II)
This is a cytosolic enzyme (whereas CPS I is mitochondrial and used for the urea cycle)
CPSI: Substrates are HCO 3 - , ammonia, 2 ATP
CPSII: Substrates are HCO 3 - , glutamine, 2 ATP
Mammalian CPS II is the committed step in pyrimidine synthesis, because carbamoyl phosphate made by CPS II has no fate other than incorporation into pyrimidines

de novo Pyrimidine Synthesis
Carbamoyl-P synthase II : inhibited by UTP
Note: Carbamoyl-P synthase I : involved in urea cycle

OMP decarboxylase
OMP decarboxylase is known for being an extraordinarily efficient enzyme
Accelerates the reaction by a factor of 10 17
Catalyzed reaction: 18 milliseconds
Uncatalyzed reaction: 78 million years
No cofactors, metals, or prosthetic groups involved

Pyrimidine degradation
Unlike Purines which end up as uric acid, pyrimidines are degraded to useful compounds
Uracil or Cytosine → → Acetyl CoA → TCA cycle
Thymine → → Succinyl CoA → TCA cycle

RNA/DNA interconversion
DNA: deoxy ribonucleotides
UDP → dUTP → → dTMP
